preFix.title: Fitting a sink overflow and waste assembly
preFix.subtitle: Fit a slotted waste, overflow plate, and bottle trap to a ceramic basin
preFix.excerpt: How to fit the complete waste and overflow assembly to a new ceramic washbasin, including the slotted waste fitting, the overflow cover plate, the connecting overflow pipe, and the bottle trap. Covers the correct order of assembly and how to achieve a watertight seal without overtightening ceramic.
preFix.sourceNotes: BS EN 31: wash-basins. CIBSE Guide G (public health engineering). Plumbers Handbook (CIPHE). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: must be fitted to any basin with an overflow; a plain unslotted waste would block the overflow outlet beneath the basin. Most waste kits include the slotted waste body, the overflow cover plate, a flexible overflow pipe, and a 32 mm bottle trap. Check all parts are present before starting. Finding a missing component after the basin is set in place is avoidable.

Basin waste kit containing: slotted waste body, rubber top seal, overflow cover plate with screw, flexible overflow pipe, back nut, 32 mm bottle trap with 32 mm outlet. PTFE tape for pipe thread joints. Silicone sealant (clear sanitary grade) to seal the waste flange to the basin.

Apply a thin bead of clear silicone sealant around the underside of the waste flange rim before fitting. Push the waste down through the basin outlet hole from above. Below the basin, slide the rubber sealing washer onto the waste thread, followed by the flat washer, and then thread the back nut on by hand. The slot in the waste body must align with the overflow outlet on the underside of the basin, this connection point is visible as a round hole in the basin underside. Tighten the back nut with the water-pump pliers until the rubber washer is compressed firmly, pulling the flange tight against the basin surface. Wipe away any silicone that squeezes out around the flange on the basin upper face.
Step 2, fit the overflow cover plate

Thread the flexible overflow pipe from below, up through the overflow hole in the basin. Fit the overflow cover plate over the pipe on the basin face and secure it with its fixing screw from the front. Do not overtighten; ceramic basins can crack if the overflow fitting is forced. Connect the lower end of the flexible overflow pipe to the slot on the side of the waste body.
Step 3, fit the bottle trap and connect to the soil pipe

Thread the bottle trap hand-tight onto the tail of the waste body. The trap outlet (the horizontal pipe leaving the side of the bottle) connects to the waste pipe in the wall. Cut the connecting pipe to length with the utility knife if it is push-fit plastic. Ensure the trap is positioned so its outlet falls slightly toward the wall pipe for drainage; a level or rising run will not drain fully. Tighten all hand-tightened plastic connections by half a turn with the adjustable spanner, no more.

Run water and check all joints. Plug the basin and fill it to just above the overflow hole to verify the overflow drains freely through the waste. Leave the trap full and check under the basin for any seeping joints after ten minutes.

attrs.fix: Drain the trap and remove the waste assembly. Clean off the old silicone, apply a fresh bead, and refit, tightening the back nut firmly.
attrs.cause: The silicone seal around the flange was insufficient, or the back nut was not tightened enough to compress the rubber washer fully.
attrs.symptom: Water drips from the waste flange joint at the basin surface

attrs.fix: Loosen the back nut and rotate the waste body until the slot aligns with the overflow outlet, then re-tighten.
attrs.cause: The slot in the waste body is not aligned with the overflow outlet port on the basin underside.
attrs.symptom: Overflow runs slowly or not at all

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
